From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Administrative division in Thailand

Not to be confused with [Tampon](/wiki/Tampon "Tampon").

***Tambon*** ([Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): [ตำบล](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E0%B8%95%E0%B8%B3%E0%B8%9A%E0%B8%A5 "wikt:ตำบล"), pronounced [[tām.bōn]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Thai "Help:IPA/Thai")) is a local governmental unit in [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"). Below [district](/wiki/District "District") (*[amphoe](/wiki/Districts_of_Thailand "Districts of Thailand")*) and [province](/wiki/Province "Province") (*[changwat](/wiki/Provinces_of_Thailand "Provinces of Thailand")*), they form the third administrative subdivision level. As of 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Tambon&action=edit) there were 7,255 tambon,[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) not including the 180 *[khwaeng](/wiki/Khwaeng "Khwaeng")* of [Bangkok](/wiki/Bangkok "Bangkok"), which are set at the same administrative level, thus every [district](/wiki/Districts_of_Thailand "Districts of Thailand") contains eight to ten tambon. *Tambon* is usually translated as "[township](/wiki/Township "Township")" or "subdistrict" in English – the latter is the recommended translation,[[3]](#cite_note-dopatranscription-3) though also often used for *[king amphoe](/wiki/Districts_of_Thailand#King_amphoe "Districts of Thailand")*, the designation for a subdistrict acting as a branch (Thai: *king*) of the parent district. Tambon are further subdivided into 74,944 villages (*[muban](/wiki/Muban "Muban")*) as of 2008.[[4]](#cite_note-4) *Tambon* within cities or towns are not subdivided into villages, but may have less formal communities called *chumchon* ([ชุมชน](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E0%B8%8A%E0%B8%B8%E0%B8%A1%E0%B8%8A%E0%B8%99 "wikt:ชุมชน")) that may be formed into [community associations](/wiki/Community_association "Community association"). The average area of a subdistrict in Thailand is about 70.72 km2 (27.31 sq mi), while its average population of a subdistrict in Thailand is about 9,637 people.

| Local divisions |
| | First-level | | --- | | * [Provincial administrative organisation](/wiki/Provincial_administrative_organization "Provincial administrative organization")  (*Ongkan borihan suan changwat*)  ---  * [Bangkok](/wiki/Bangkok "Bangkok")†  (*Krung Thep Maha Nakhon*) | | Second-level | | * [City municipality](/wiki/Thesaban#City_municipality "Thesaban")  (*Thesaban nakhon*)  ---  * [Town municipality](/wiki/Thesaban#Town_municipality "Thesaban")  (*Thesaban mueang*)  ---  * [Subdistrict municipality](/wiki/Thesaban#Subdistrict_municipality "Thesaban")  (*Thesaban tambon*)  ---  * Subdistrict administrative organisation  (*Ongkan borihan suan tambon*)  ---  * [Pattaya City](/wiki/Pattaya_City "Pattaya City")†  (*Mueang Pattaya*) | | Subdivisions of Bangkok | | * [District](/wiki/List_of_districts_of_Bangkok "List of districts of Bangkok") (*Khet*) * [Subdistrict](/wiki/Khwaeng "Khwaeng") (*Khwaeng*) | | Subdivisions of City municipality | | * *[Khwaeng](/wiki/Khwaeng "Khwaeng")* | |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Bang_Bai_Mai_TAO_office.JPG/250px-Bang_Bai_Mai_TAO_office.JPG)](/wiki/File:Bang_Bai_Mai_TAO_office.JPG)

Office of TAO Bang Bai Mai, Surat Thani

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tambon&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The *tambon* as a subdivision has a long history. It was the second-level subdivision of the area administered by a provincial town in the 19th century. The governor of the province was supposed to appoint a communal elder, *[kamnan](/wiki/Kamnan "Kamnan")* or *phan*.

In the administrative reforms started in 1892 under Prince [Damrong Rajanubhab](/wiki/Damrong_Rajanubhab "Damrong Rajanubhab"), the first Thai Minister of [the Interior](/wiki/Ministry_of_Interior_(Thailand) "Ministry of Interior (Thailand)"), the three levels of subdivision of provinces were continued, i.e., starting from [district](/wiki/Amphoe "Amphoe") to *tambon* to the lowest level called *muban*.

### Muban

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tambon&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Muban")]

Main article: [Muban](/wiki/Muban "Muban")

The subdistricts are subdivided into administrative villages (muban, หมู่บ้าน) as the lowest administrative subdivision. Usually these are referred to much more often by the village number than the actual name, especially as an administrative village may contain more than one settlement, or a large settlement may be split into more than one administrative village. One of the elected village headmen is elected as the subdistrict headman ([Kamnan](/wiki/Kamnan "Kamnan")).

### Subdistrict (Tambon) administrative organization (SAO/TAO)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tambon&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Subdistrict (Tambon) administrative organization (SAO/TAO)")]

With the *Tambon Council and Tambon Administrative Authority Act BE 2537* (1994)[[5]](#cite_note-5) and later by the [constitution of 1997](/wiki/1997_constitution_of_Thailand "1997 constitution of Thailand"), *tambon* were decentralized into local government units with an elected tambon council. Depending on its size and tax income a *tambon* may either be administered by a *Subdistrict (Tambon) Administrative Organization* (SAO or TAO, [Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): องค์การบริหารส่วนตำบล) or a *Tambon Council* (TC, [Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): สภาตำบล). However, since 2001 all of the Tambon Councils have been upgraded to Tambon Administrative Organizations. The TAO council consist of two representatives from each administrative village in the subdistrict, and one directly elected president. The subdistrict area which belongs to a municipality (*[thesaban](/wiki/Thesaban "Thesaban")*) is administered by the municipal council. In the event only part of the subdistrict is within a municipality, the remaining part is administered by a TAO. Adjoining subdistricts of a single district can also have a joint TAO.

### One Tambon One Product

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tambon&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: One Tambon One Product")]

Main article: [One Tambon One Product](/wiki/One_Tambon_One_Product "One Tambon One Product")

In 2001, Thai Prime Minister [Thaksin Shinawatra](/wiki/Thaksin_Shinawatra "Thaksin Shinawatra") started a project in which every *tambon* would select a typical, distinctive local product. The project then aids in promoting the product, as well as assisting in modernizing production. Shops selling OTOP products are located in each provincial capital.
